This line is failing on Python 3:

```
TypeError: string argument without an encoding
```

I think it's the call to `bytearray` that's the issue. PdfString is defined as a subclass of `str`, which means unicode on Python 3. So passing it to `bytearray` is implicitly trying to encode unicode to bytes, and it refuses to do that without an explicit encoding.

Either the decoding machinery needs to use strings, as it did before, or `PdfString` would need to inherit from `bytes` instead of `str`.

$ cp route.gpx route-bom.gpx

> from lxml import etree

>     dom = etree.XML(f.read())
> with open('route-bom.gpx', 'rb') as f:

and compared them using [this function](http://stackoverflow.com/a/24349916).

> For lxml it seems that it is converting back to bytes in line 101.

Hmm, you're right. I think this stuff is mostly compatibility for the `minixml` implementation, which unfortunately takes a `str` instead of `bytes`. But doing a double decode->encode is not the best choice.

I think firstly, this function should be modified to pick one or the other (preferably `bytes`) and secondly, all the special decoding can be moved into the `minixml` implementation.

I prefixed this attribute with an underscore so that both test cases pass whether datastore was passed to `__init__` or `init_app`. The issue with it being named `datastore` is that that takes precedence over calls to `Security.__getattr__()`:

```python
def test_access_datastore_from_factory(app, datastore):
    security = Security()  # sets Security.datastore to None
    security.init_app(app, datastore)  # sets Security._state['datastore'] to datastore

    assert security.datastore is not None  # fails, because this retrieves Security.datastore, not Security.__getattr__('datastore'), which would return Security._state['datastore']
```

The problem here is not the concurrent transactions, but resilience to faults (e.g. power outages, OOM kill).

If a user calls `channel_batch_close` with a list of two channels, and the process is killed after the first channel is closed but prior to sending the transaction to the second, we end up in a bad situation. There will be no information in the node's log explaining "why" the channel was closed, and the second channel will not be closed on restart.

To fix this we must go through the write-ahead-log machinery and close the channels on the event handler. The WAL guarantees that either we start the operation and retry on restart or don't start it at all.

Note: `ActionChannelClose` doesn't working with batches of channels, so it's not perfect, but making it work in batches is another problem.

One can be shown to exist at this point, on the assumption that we're not in the abnormal regime where the connection count, counting the new conn, is greater than `max_in_flight`.  If we are in the abnormal regime, trying to set a non-zero `RDY` on the new conn is going to fail and we have to rely on periodic redistribution to give `RDY` to the new conn.
